content.problemStatement:
In Chefland, there are NNN cities (numbered 111 through NNN). Initially, there are no roads between them. The Mayor of Chefland has ordered engineers to build roads across Chefland in such a way that each city is reachable from all other cities.

For each pair of distinct cities iii and jjj, the cost of building a road between these cities is equal to (i∧ji \wedge ji∧j), where ∧\wedge∧ denotes the bitwise AND operation. However, leaders of the cities have a weird condition ― they only allow roads with positive costs to be built from their cities.

Help the Mayor find the minimum cost of building roads in the required way or determine that it is impossible.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 10^9).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum possible total cost to make all cities mutually reachable using only roads with positive cost, or -1 if it is impossible.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=3 the cities are {1,2,3}. The cheapest edges are 1‑3 (cost 1) and 2‑3 (cost 2); total = 3.\nFor N=5 the sum of lowest set bits from 2 to 5 is 2+1+4+1 = 8.

solution.approach:
For a road between cities i and j the cost is i & j. A road can be built only if i & j > 0, i.e., i and j share at least one common set bit. The graph formed by connecting all pairs that share a bit is connected unless N is a power of two (the highest power‑of‑two city has a unique most‑significant bit and cannot connect to any other city). Hence, if N is a power of two the answer is -1.

If the graph is connected we can build a spanning tree. For each city i (i ≥ 2) the smallest possible positive cost of an incident edge equals the value of its lowest set bit (lsb(i) = i & -i). This value can always be achieved by connecting i to another city that also contains this bit (such a city always exists because N is not a power of two). Using Kruskal’s algorithm, picking for every vertex the cheapest edge that connects it to the already built component yields a minimum spanning tree whose total weight is Σ_{i=2..N} lsb(i).

Thus the problem reduces to computing S(N) = Σ_{i=2..N} lsb(i). The count of numbers whose lsb equals 2^k is floor(N/2^k) - floor(N/2^{k+1}). Therefore:
    S(N) = ( Σ_{k≥0} 2^k * (⌊N/2^k⌋ - ⌊N/2^{k+1}⌋) ) - 1   (subtract lsb(1)=1).
We iterate over k while 2^k ≤ N (at most 30 iterations for N ≤ 10^9) and accumulate the sum. The overall complexity per test case is O(log N) time and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * log N) – at most about 30 operations per test case.
